You get a crack tough, you call him and you leave a message because he goes from track to track, right? And you can get him for like five grand if it's an emergency and he'll drop what he's doing and come. Otherwise, you might wait three or four days until he's back in Ohio from Pennsylvania or he's in Pennsylvania instead of in New Jersey or whatever. Sometimes there's a waiting list, sometimes I can't get to you for two weeks, you do this in the meantime. Hey, the guy is like booked beyond belief. We all believe he's the only guy who can do that. And I've watched him do it. I know I couldn't do it, but I think probably any blacksmith who paid attention could figure it out two or three times and he'll sell you the glob. You can go to the website and buy the glob, but we are all convinced. He is the only guy who can do this and get it right. So we will wait, we will fly him in, we will do what we got to do to get him. It is useful to have people believe that. It is not particularly difficult to have people believe that. They actually want to believe that. And that's important to understand. See now they found you, they got you, they want to believe you're unique. That's like important to them. So they pretty much will convince themselves you're the only one who can do the one thing that is your greatest core competency if you just sort of encourage them in it. But once they believe it, they're very dependent on you. My experience at Guthrie Ranker is sort of interesting. The first thing I did for Guthrie Ranker was I did some tweaks to the offer of the Think and Grow Rich Info Martial, which was their first show. And it was dying. And it had a good run, but it was starting to do this. And I did a couple things and it went back up like this and it continued for a period of time and everybody made a lot of money. The next show they did was Personal Power One, the first Tony Robbins show. And I didn't do much. But I rewrote the CTA and I made a couple of suggestions. And I was on the project team getting paid consulting fees to issue opinions about everything from which of these three testimonials do you like to, you know, that sort of stuff. Well, so Think and Grow Rich came back to life. And Personal Power was, of course, a huge hit. Quite frankly, Personal Power would have been a huge hit whether I'd been dead alive here, not here, making any efforts. Personal Power was a huge hit because they surrounded them with celebrities. They did a pretty good show. And Tony's a phenomenal salesperson. However, having done those two things and had two successes in a row in a field in which they had no experience or expertise. They come to the conclusion that I'm the rabbit foot. We don't dare do a show without Kennedy. We're not exactly sure what the hell contribution he's making. But we're pretty sure we ought to at least rub the master on his head before we send it off to the stations. Now, they wouldn't enunciate it that way. And I wouldn't enunciate it that way in front of them. But fundamentally, that's like where we were. So I'm like the rabbit foot guy. For a long time, nothing happened over there without me like blessing it. In many cases, I didn't do much. But I got paid to, yep, that makes sense. Rub it here. Go. Well, hundreds of thousands of dollars, many hundreds of thousands of dollars. Nobody wanted to do a project beating without me there. Half the time. Nothing happened in the project meeting. Meetings are meetings. 12 people go. They eat bagels. They eat fruit. Everybody sits around and talks. Nothing happens. You could just as easily as sent me a recording in the project meeting. I could have looked, no, no, no, I've got to be there. We'll reschedule a project meeting so you can be here. You've got to be here. Why? Because I want to be sitting there. They convinced themselves that somehow, some way, I was doing something that nobody else could do. That's a dependency issue. Second one, there's something mystical about what you do. So Harvey Dent's proprietary software that he uses to calculate the trends thing, your proprietary analytic process for finding the eight unexploited opportunities, your interview strategy, your something. And it's why they can't see you do everything that you do. It's a mistake like to be visible doing everything. Because a lot of what most of us do, if you see it, ain't nothing to see. It's not all that impressive. So you only want to really see an impressive stuff.
